justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Distributed tracing gap explicitly identified as cross-service diagnosis blocker — called 'the single biggest observability gap' and escalated. (2) 30-second log delay called out as 'debugging 30 seconds behind reality' during active incidents. (3) Stale runbook flagged as 'STALE AND DANGEROUS' with detailed reasoning about why outdated runbooks are worse than none. (4) No recovery tooling for stuck orders identified — highlighted that manual DB surgery is the only path. (5) Multiple specific recommendations: distributed tracing, transactional outbox pattern, state timeout mechanism, runbook refresh, stuck-order metrics/alerts, admin recovery tooling. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough incident response assessment — goes well beyond monitoring coverage to evaluate debugging capability, recovery paths, and on-call experience
Identifies the subtle stuck-order blind spot: orders stuck in PROCESSING produce zero failures, so existing alerts never fire — this is a deeper insight than the rubric required
Strong framing of the 30-second log delay as 'making decisions on stale data' rather than a minor latency issue
Runbook assessment is detailed — notes it may reference infrastructure that no longer exists and likely lacks stuck-order remediation steps
Identifies additional gaps beyond rubric requirements: missing circuit breakers, no idempotency guarantees, no timeout configuration, retry storm risk
Correctly notes PagerDuty routing is adequate (alert threshold concern is nuanced rather than dismissive)
Output is extremely verbose — while thorough, the signal-to-noise ratio could be improved for a time-pressured incident readiness review

agent_output_excerpt: {"integration_assessment":{"component_interactions":[{"from":"order_state_machine","to":"downstream_consumers","status":"risk","issue":"No event log or outbox pattern means downstream services have no way to know about state transitions that failed silently or partially committed.
